---
title: "Yang-Mills Lattice Gauge Theory - William's Explorations"

bibliography: references.bib

# format:
#   html:
#     code-fold: true
# jupyter: python3
---

### April 12, 2026

Smooth immersions are maps whose differentials
are injective everywhere. Smooth embeddings are 
injective smooth immersions that are also 
homeomorphisms onto their images. Smooth immersions
and embeddings are key ingredients in the theory of
submanifolds. 

The rank of a linear map $L: V \to W$ is the dimension
of its range: $\dim L(V)$. Given two linear maps
$L_1, L_2$, we can find two change of basis 
maps $B_1, B_2$ such that $L_1 = B_1 \circ L_2 \circ B_2$
if and only if the rank of $L_1$ and $L_2$ is the same.

The rank of a smooth map between manifolds
$F: M \to N$ at a point $p$
is the rank of the differential of that smooth
map at the point $p$. 

A linear map is injective if and only if its kernel 
is the $\{0\}$ space. Equivalently, a linear map
$L: V \to W$ is injective if and only if
$\dim L(V) = \dim V$, because then no dimensions
are left for the kernel. 

A smooth map has constant rank if its rank at every point
on the manifold is the same. A smooth map $F$ of 
constant rank also has full rank
if the rank of its differential at all points
is as large as possible: either equal to the dimension
of its domain or codomain. 
By logic, smooth immersions are 
smooth maps of constant rank and 
of full rank whose rank is equal to 
the dimension of the tangent space of the domain manifold.

The mapping $\iota: SU(2) \to \mathbb C^{2 \times 2} \cong
\mathbb R^8$ is smooth provided that for every coordinate system
$\xi$ in $SU(2)$ and $\eta$ in $\mathbb C^{2 \times 2}$, the coordinate
expression $\eta \circ \iota \circ \xi^{-1}$ is Euclidean smooth
and defined on an open set of $\mathbb R^3$.

We have $C^{\infty}(M) = \{f: M \to \mathbb R: f \text{ smooth in the 
sense of manifold theory} \}$. 

Let $M$ be a topological space and let $N \subset M$. 
The subspace topology on $N$ is the collection: 
$\{ N \cap U: U \text{ is open in } M\}$.

Let $E$ be a module over a commutative ring $R$. 
Let $g: E \times E \to R$ be a map. 
If $g$ is bilinear, we call $g$ a symmetric 
form if $g(x,y) = g(y,x)$ for all $x,y \in E$.
If $R$ has an automorphism of order 2
written $a \mapsto \overline{a}$, we say $g$
is a Hermitian form if it is linear in its 
first variable, antilinear in its second, and 
$g(x,y) = \overline{g(y,x)}$. In either case, we call
$g$ a scalar product.

What is a left-invariant vector field on $SU(2)$?
Why does the tangent space 
$T_A SU(2) = A \cdot \mathfrak{su}(2)$
for $A \in SU(2)$?

Let $\mathfrak{X}(M)$ denote the set of all 
smooth vector fields on $M$. Note that 
$C^\infty(M)$ is a commutative ring, 
with addition given by pointwise addition
of functions, and multiplication given by pointwise
multiplication of functions. It is
not a field because a smooth function
which takes the value $0$ somewhere 
is not invertible in the sense 
of multiplication. Then $\mathfrak{X}(M)$
is a $C^\infty(M)$-module. To verify this, think
about why $\mathfrak{X}(M)$ is a group
under pointwise addition. Then think about
how a smooth function $f \in C^{\infty}(M)$ acts
on a vector field, and think about why $(f + g)V =
fV + gV$ follows from pointwise considerations.
This module has a basis given by
the smooth sections
$(\partial_i)_{i = 1}^n$. So 
the coefficients in a linear combination would be smooth
functions on $M$.

A derivation on $C^{\infty}(M)$ is a function
$D: C^{\infty}(M) \to C^{\infty}(M)$ that is 
$\mathbb R$-linear and Leibnizian. 
If $V \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$ is a vector field, 
then the map $\Psi: C^{\infty}(M) \ni f \mapsto Vf \in C^{\infty}(M)$
is a derivation.
Let's check that $\Psi$ is Leibnizian. 
Let $f,g \in C^{\infty}(M)$. 
Fix $p \in M$. Then $V_p \in T_p M$. By definition 
of tangent vector at $p$, we note that 
$\Psi(fg)(p) = V_p(fg) = f(p) V_p(g) + V_p(f) g(p) 
= f(p) \Psi(g)(p) + \Psi(f)(p) g(p),$
so the Leibnizian property holds pointwise. 

Every derivation comes from a vector field.
Fix a derivation $D: C^{\infty}(M) \to C^{\infty}(M)$.
Notice that when $p$ is a point on the manifold $M$, then
$C^{\infty}(M) \ni f \mapsto (Df)(p) \in \mathbb R$ 
if a rule assigning functions to real numbers. 
Recall that a vector field $V \in \mathfrak{X}(M)$, 
at each point on the manifold, assigns functions
to real numbers. So we suppose that
if $V$ does exist, then it should satisfy
$V_p f = (Df)(p)$. The class of smooth functions are
rich enough to determine $V_p$ based on this requirement.

How do we view $Vf$ as a smooth function when $V$ is a
vector field and when $f$ is a smooth function?

How can we consider vector fields as derivations 
$C^{\infty}(M) \to C^{\infty}(M)$?

### April 9, 2026

Consider section 1.4 in @chatterjee2026.
 
- Question: Can you write down a formula
for the leading order term of the free energy using
techniques from @Chatterjee2016?

Work on this question especially when my 
log-gases talk is over. (That is, in May).
Quickly resolving this question would
mean solving it by mid-May, after a couple
of weeks of working on it.

Outline until September 1, 2026. 

1. Go through Section 11 and Section 17 of @Chatterjee2016 
step by step by the end of April.
2. As soon as this is finished, think about how 
to summarize the paper as an introduction section 
to my thesis. Work on this in May.
3. The rest of the thesis
will just be filling in the details of @Chatterjee2016's 
paper in my own voice. Work
on this in May, June, and July.

Thus, the thesis should be finished by the end of July,
more or less, with August to spare.

Also for April: prepare my log-gases talk on May 7th.

July will be busy with the CQFT exam preparation.
April, May, and June will also require some work with
the CQFT course, but less work than the seminar.
